titleEn: By Your Life, Umayya Did Not Bequeath
translationEn: By your life, Umayya did not bequeath to his kin a testament like that which Jacob bequeathed. He counseled them, as he turned away departing, with sin before God and transgression. O my sons! If you attempt to steal, then take pickaxes, all of them perforated. And come to people's houses from behind, until every one of them is broken through.
context: A satirical poem mocking the Banu Umayya, comparing their patriarch's legacy unfavorably with the righteous testament of the Prophet Jacob (Ya'qub). Hassan ridicules their forefathers as having taught their descendants treachery and theft rather than virtue.

titleEn: Convey to Usayd a Message from Me
translationEn: Come, convey to Usayd a message from me: your maternal uncle is a slave tested by drink. By your life, Usayd was never loyal to his neighbor, nor was Khalid, nor the son of the broad-armored woman Zaynab. And Uttab is a slave who does not fulfill his pledge, a liar with a curly-haired, disciplined head.
context: A hija poem targeting Usayd and his kinsmen, accusing them of disloyalty, broken pledges, and ignoble lineage. Hassan attacks their character by impugning their maternal bloodlines and personal vices, a standard technique of pre-Islamic and early Islamic satirical poetry.

titleEn: When Umm Amr Saw Me She Turned Away
translationEn: When Umm Amr saw me, she turned away, having swallowed her dismay at my old age, and wrapped herself tightly in her garment.
context: A brief ghazal fragment in which Hassan, now old, describes a woman's reaction upon seeing his aged appearance. The single verse captures a poignant moment of rejection, reflecting the poet's awareness of his advancing years.

titleEn: Who Will Carry the Rhymes After Hassan and His Son?

verses.ajuz: ومن للمثاني بعد زيد بن ثابت

translationEn: Who will champion the rhymes after Hassan and his son? And who will carry the paired verses of the Quran after Zayd ibn Thabit?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Zayd ibn Thabit, the scribe of the Prophet
Hassan ibn Thabit, the poet himself

context: A boastful verse in which Hassan places himself as the unrivaled master of poetry, just as Zayd ibn Thabit was the irreplaceable scribe and compiler of the Quran. Both men were from the Ansar of Medina, and Hassan links the two great cultural legacies of the Medinan community.

titleEn: Hakim Was Saved at Badr by His Horse's Gallop

verses.sadr: كم فيهم من ماجد ذي سورة ،
أو كل أروع ماجد ذي مرة ،

translationEn: Hakim was saved at Badr only by his horse's gallop, fleeing like a colt from the mares of al-A'waj. He cast away his weapons and fled unguarded, slipping like a silk-weaver upon his loom. When he saw Badr's battlefield gleaming with battalions of Aws or Khazraj — patient warriors who lead the brave to their fates, marching on the broad, clear road. How many a glorious man of fierce valor was among them, a champion in the most perilous of places! And a chieftain who gives lavishly with his hand, bearer of the heavy blood-money, crowned with honor. Or every bold and glorious man of strength, or every armor-clad warrior with a loose sword-belt. And Huwayris, son of the red-bottomed woman, escaped too, his brain boiling like smelted brass.
context: A poem satirizing the Quraysh fighters who fled at the Battle of Badr (624 CE), particularly Hakim ibn Hizam and Huwayris. Hassan contrasts their cowardly flight with the valor of the Ansar warriors of Aws and Khazraj who fought alongside the Prophet. This is one of Hassan's most vivid Badr poems.

titleEn: Tall of Sword-Belt, Lofty of Tent-Pole
translationEn: Tall of sword-belt, lofty of tent-pole, the pure essence of noble lineage from the Khazraj.
context: A brief boast celebrating Khazrajite nobility. The verse uses classic Arab praise imagery: a tall sword-belt denotes physical stature, and a lofty tent-pole signifies generous hospitality. Hassan proudly asserts that he is the purest extract of Khazrajite lineage.

titleEn: Tell Rabi'a and His Cousin Nawfal

verses.ajuz: أني مصيب العظم ، إن لم أصفح

translationEn: Tell Rabi'a and his cousin Nawfal that I strike to the bone if I do not choose to pardon. I am like a fierce lion of the thicket, prowling the rocky ground through the wide mountain passes. His mate went hungry, and he spent a night without provision, so he is wrathful unless he draws blood. You would think him Hassan when I provoked him to war — so leave the open ground for your narrow refuge and make room! Treachery, deceit, obscenity, and baseness have all taken up dwelling in al-Abtah. A people whose assembly follows obscenity whenever it speaks, and the reformer's counsel is wasted. And at the Hijr, every worthless cur splits off — if he cannot bark properly at the debate, he just yelps.
context: A fierce hija poem directed at Rabi'a and Nawfal of Quraysh. Hassan compares himself to a hungry lion and warns his enemies that his poetic attacks strike to the bone. He accuses the Meccan elite of harboring treachery and obscenity in al-Abtah, the valley near Mecca where the Quraysh gathered.

titleAr: يا دوس ، إن أبا أزيهر أصبحت
titleEn: O Daws, Abu Uzayhir's Echoes Are Gone

verses.sadr: يا دوس ، إن أبا أزيهر أصبحت

translationEn: O Daws, Abu Uzayhir's echoes have become hostage to the wasteland — so strike the fire of war! A war that would turn children grey-haired, for only every servile wretch stoops to dishonor. Weep for your brother with every supple brown spear, and with every white sword gleaming like agate, broad-bladed. And with every bright-skinned mare, as though she were a swooping eagle that glides and soars. And a fleet horse smooth of gait, like a wolf in an empty, wide-open plain. If you kill a hundred for him, it is a paltry price compared to Abu Uzayhir among the men of al-Abtah.
context: An elegy and call to arms addressed to the tribe of Daws after the killing of Abu Uzayhir, a nobleman murdered by men of Quraysh. Hassan incites the Daws to avenge their kinsman, declaring that even a hundred of the men of al-Abtah would be a cheap price for the blood of Abu Uzayhir.

titleEn: The Banu Asad Have Failed

verses.sadr: ونجا ابن قيس في بقية قومه ،

translationEn: The Banu Asad have failed, and their mighty one returned on the day of the Well with disgrace and shame. Among them Abu al-'Asi fell headlong, struck down from atop a swift, sure-footed mare. And the man Rum'a they left behind, his chest bleeding with a gushing, flowing wound. And Ibn Qays escaped with the remnant of his people, his nose's bridge defiled with pus.
context: A poem celebrating the Muslim victory at Badr and mocking the Banu Asad and their Qurayshi allies who were killed or fled. Hassan names specific enemies who fell or were disgraced, a classic technique of hija poetry meant to humiliate the defeated and commemorate the Muslim triumph.

titleEn: Al-Awwam Only Insulted Me Because

verses.ajuz: أخو سمك في البحر جار التماسح

translationEn: Al-Awwam only insulted me because he is like a fish in the sea, neighbor to the crocodiles. A base, lowly, obscene man and son of an obscene man, ignoble of roots, his origin remote and questionable. He has a wine-jug in his house and a tavern in which he sells, so he is always drunk and reeking.
context: A sharp satirical poem against al-Awwam, whom Hassan attacks for his ignoble lineage and vice of wine-selling. By calling him a fish among crocodiles, Hassan implies he is a small, insignificant creature out of his depth. The accusation of wine-selling was particularly damning in the early Islamic context.

titleEn: Radiant, Upon Him the Seal of Prophethood

verses.sadr: وضم الإله اسم النبي إلى اسمه ،
verses.ajuz: إذا قال في الخمس المؤذن أشهد
من الرسل ، والأوثان في الأرض تعبد
بذلك ما عمرت فيا لناس أشهد

verses.sadr: تعاليت رب الناس عن قول من دعا

translationEn: Radiant, upon him is the seal of prophethood from God, witnessed and manifest, gleaming and testifying. God joined the Prophet's name to His own name: when the muezzin says 'I bear witness' in the five prayers. He derived for him a name from His own to honor him: the Lord of the Throne is Mahmud (the Praised), and this one is Muhammad (the Praiseworthy). A prophet who came to us after despair and a gap between messengers, while idols were worshipped on the earth. He became a shining lamp and a guide, gleaming like a polished Indian sword. He warned us of the Fire, gave glad tidings of Paradise, and taught us Islam — so we praise God. You are the God of creation, my Lord and my Creator; to that I bear witness before mankind as long as I live. Exalted are You, Lord of mankind, above the words of those who call upon any other as god — You are the Most High and Most Glorious. Yours is all creation and all blessings, and all authority is Yours; from You alone we seek guidance, and You alone we worship.
context: One of Hassan's most celebrated praise poems for the Prophet Muhammad. The famous third verse about the derivation of the name Muhammad from God's attribute Mahmud became one of the most quoted lines in Arabic literature. The poem is a declaration of faith as much as a panegyric, moving from praise of the Prophet to direct worship of God.

titleEn: Clad in Rings of Iron They Are Led

verses.sadr: ماض على الهول ، ركاب لما قطعوا ،

verses.ajuz: ما قال كان قضاء غير مردود

translationEn: Clad in rings of iron mail, they are led by one tough of fiber, resolute, no coward — I mean the Messenger, for God has favored him above all creation with piety and generosity. You claimed you would protect your territory, and that the water of Badr would never be reached. But we reached it and heeded not your words, until we drank our fill, not a mere sip. Holding fast to a rope that never breaks, firmly tied to a rope of God, extended. Among us is the Messenger, and among us is the truth we follow until death, and a victory without limit. He presses on through terror, mounting whatever danger they face, when the brave warriors flinch before the champions. Faithful and resolute, a blazing star by which men find light, a full moon shining upon all the glorious ones. Blessed, his face like the radiance of the full moon — whatever he speaks becomes a decree that cannot be reversed.
context: A powerful madih poem praising the Prophet and the Muslim warriors at Badr. Hassan addresses the Quraysh directly, reminding them that they boasted no one would reach Badr's wells, yet the Muslims drank their fill. The poem glorifies the Prophet as a divinely guided leader whose every word is an irrevocable decree.

titleEn: By God My Lord, We Shall Not Forsake the Noble One
translationEn: By God my Lord, we shall not forsake a noble one — pure of character, glorious in his ancestry. A generous man calling to the Lord Most High, offering sincere counsel, raising the pillars of faith. Like the crescent moon, blessed and full of mercy, easy of nature and sweet of disposition. If you abandon him, then my Lord is able — He has been returning the visitors with His bounty. By God my Lord, we shall not depart from his command so long as there is life hoped for and a return to the Hereafter. We seek no lord besides Him as our protector, until we arrive at the morning of the appointed Day.
context: A poem of loyalty to the Prophet Muhammad, composed during the early period of Islam when the Prophet faced opposition. Hassan pledges that the Ansar will never abandon the Prophet, comparing him to the crescent moon in his blessedness. The poem is both a declaration of faith and a political statement of the Ansar's unwavering support.

titleAr: لقد خاب قوم غاب عنهم نبيهم ،
titleEn: Truly Lost Are the People from Whom Their Prophet Departed

verses.sadr: لقد خاب قوم غاب عنهم نبيهم ،
لقد نزلت منه على اهل يثرب
نبي يرى ما لا يرى الناس حوله ،
verses.ajuz: ويتلو كتاب اله في كل مسجد

verses.sadr: وإن قال في يوم مقالة غائب ،
verses.ajuz: فتصديقها في اليوم أو في ضحى الغد

translationEn: Truly lost are the people from whom their prophet departed, and blessed are those to whom he journeys morning and evening. He left a people whose minds had gone astray, and alighted upon a people with a renewed light. Their Lord guided them through him after their misguidance, and set them right — whoever follows the truth is rightly guided. Can the error of a people who acted foolishly in blindness be equal to those guided by a guide? Upon the people of Yathrib there descended from him a caravan of guidance, alighting upon them most auspiciously. A prophet who sees what the people around him cannot see, and recites the Book of God in every mosque. If he speaks a word one day about something unseen, its confirmation comes that very day or by the next morning. May Abu Bakr rejoice in his good fortune through his companionship — whoever God makes blessed is truly blessed.
context: A poem celebrating the Hijra (migration) of the Prophet from Mecca to Medina. Hassan contrasts the loss of Quraysh, who rejected the Prophet, with the blessing received by the people of Yathrib (Medina). The final verse praises Abu Bakr al-Siddiq for his companionship during the migration, making this an important historical poem about the Hijra.

titleAr: ألم تر أن الغدر واللؤم والخنا
titleEn: Do You Not See That Treachery and Baseness

verses.sadr: ألم تر أن الغدر واللؤم والخنا
verses.ajuz: بنى مسكنا بين المعين إلى عرد
إلى بيت زماراء ، تلدا على تلد

verses.sadr: فقلت ولم أملك : أعمرو بن عامر
لقد شاب رأسي ، أو دنا لمشيبه ،
verses.ajuz: وما عتقت سعد بن زر ولا هند

translationEn: Do you not see that treachery, baseness, and obscenity have built a dwelling between al-Ma'in and Ard? Then Gaza, then al-Marwat, then al-Khabt, then al-Muna, all the way to the house of Zimmara, generation upon generation. I said, unable to restrain myself: Is Amr ibn Amir's scion killed for a mere slave? My head has indeed turned grey, or is near to greying, and neither Sa'd ibn Zurr nor Hind have been freed from dishonor.
context: A satirical poem lamenting that treachery has taken root across the land, listing place names to show its spread. Hassan expresses outrage at the killing of a noble man of the lineage of Amr ibn Amir to avenge a slave, and laments that certain parties remain unpunished despite his growing old waiting for justice.

titleEn: In Tayba There Is a Trace of the Messenger

verses.sadr: ولا تنمحي الآيات من دار حرمة
verses.ajuz: بها منبر الهادي الذي كان يصعد

verses.sadr: معالم لم تطمس على العهد آيها

verses.ajuz: وقبرا به واراه في الترب ملحد

verses.sadr: وما بلغت من كل أمر عشيره ،
verses.ajuz: ولكن نفسي بعض ما فيه تحمد
على طلل القبر الذي فيه أحمد
ومن قد بكته الأرض فالناس أكمد
وقد كان ذا نور ، يغور وينجد

verses.sadr: يدل على الرحمن من يقتدي به ،
وإن ناب أمر لم يقوموا بحمده ،
فبينا هم في نعمة الله بينهم
عزيز عليه أن يحيدوا عن الهدى ،
فبينا هم في ذلك النور ، إذ غدا
verses.ajuz: إلى نورهم سهم من الموت مقصد
لغيبة ما كانت من الوحي تعهد

verses.sadr: فبكي رسول الله يا عين عبرة
ومالك لا تبكين ذا النعمة التي

verses.ajuz: إذا ضن معطاء بما كان يتلد

verses.sadr: وأكرم حيا في البيوت ، إذا انتمى ،

verses.ajuz: فلا العلم محبوس ، ولا الرأي يفند
من الناس ، إلا عازب العقل مبعد
لعلي به في جنة الخلد أخلد
وفي نيل ذاك اليوم أسعى وأجهد

translationEn: In Tayba there is a trace of the Messenger and a radiant landmark, though traces may fade and grow cold. Yet the signs shall not be erased from this sacred abode, where the pulpit of the Guide who used to ascend still stands. Clear signs and lasting landmarks remain, and a dwelling where he had a prayer place and a mosque. In it are chambers in whose midst he used to dwell, where a light from God was kindled and gave illumination. Landmarks whose signs have not been effaced over time — decay has come to them, yet the signs are ever renewed. I recognized there the trace of the Messenger and his covenant, and a grave in which a niche in the earth concealed him. I stayed there weeping for the Messenger, and my eyes were helped to weep, and eyes like them shed tears as well. I try to remember the blessings of the Messenger, but I find my soul unable to enumerate them, and my spirit grows numb. Stricken with grief, consumed by the loss of Ahmad, I kept counting the blessings of the Messenger. Yet I could not reach a tenth of any matter, but my soul praises even the part it can grasp. Long she stood, the eye shedding tears with all its might, over the ruins of the grave in which Ahmad lies. Blessed are you, O grave of the Messenger, and blessed is the land in which the rightly-guided one dwells. And blessed is the niche that shelters one so pure, upon which is a structure of flat stones, neatly arranged. Hands and weeping eyes heap earth upon him, and even the most fortunate were overcome with grief at that. They buried forbearance, knowledge, and mercy the evening they covered him with earth, without a pillow. They departed in sorrow, their prophet no longer among them, their backs and arms weakened. They weep for the one for whom the heavens wept that day, and for whom the earth wept — the people are in deepest grief. Has the calamity of any who perished ever equaled the calamity of the day Muhammad died? On that day the abode of revelation was cut off from them, though it had been a light traversing valley and highland. He guided to the Most Merciful all who followed him, and rescued from the terrors of disgrace and showed the right path. A leader for them, striving to guide them to truth, a teacher of sincerity — if they obey him, they prosper. Forgiving of faults, accepting their excuses, and if they do good, God is even more generous with good. If a matter befell them they could not bear with gratitude, from him came the easing of what was difficult. While they were in God's blessing among them, with a guide through whom the clear path is followed — grievous it was to him that they stray from guidance, eager that they stand firm and be rightly guided — compassionate toward them, never folding his wing but sheltering them and smoothing their way — while they were in that light, there came an arrow of death aimed at their light. He became the praised one, returning to God, mourned by the eyelids of the Messengers and glorified. The lands of the sanctuary became desolate in their expanse, bereft of the revelation they used to receive. Empty wastelands, save for the inhabited grave visited by the lost one — Balat and Gharqad weep for him. And his mosque — the desolate places mourn his absence, empty where once he had a standing-place and a seat. And at the great Jamra, then homes and courtyards, dwellings and birthplace grew desolate for him. So weep for the Messenger of God, O eye, with tears, and let me never know your tears to freeze. Why do you not weep for the possessor of the blessing whose covering grace envelops all mankind? Be generous upon him with tears and wail aloud for the loss of one whose like shall never be found. Those who came before never lost one like Muhammad, nor shall his like be lost until the Day of Resurrection. Most chaste and most faithful to covenant after covenant, and nearest of all in his generosity, never failing. More lavish than any with new wealth and old, when even the generous one hoards what he has inherited. Noblest of the living in lineage when ancestry is traced, and noblest of grandfathers — an Abtahi who ruled with authority. Most impregnable of peaks, most firmly established in glory — pillars of honor, towering and built high. Most firmly rooted in branch and origin, and a tree on the day of rain — the tree most supple. His Lord raised him from childhood and brought him to full perfection upon the noblest of virtues, a Lord most glorified. The affairs of the Muslims reached their culmination through his hand — knowledge was not imprisoned, nor was judgment unsound. I say this, and no one shall find fault with my words except one bereft of mind and far from sense. My passion shall not cease praising him, that perhaps through it I may dwell forever in the Garden of Eternity, in the company of the Chosen One — I hope through that for his neighborly closeness, and in attaining that Day I strive and exert myself.
context: This is Hassan ibn Thabit's greatest and most celebrated elegy for the Prophet Muhammad, composed after his death in 632 CE. At 46 verses, it is one of the longest elegies in early Arabic poetry. The poem is a masterwork of ritha (elegy), beginning with the sacred landmarks of Medina, moving through the Prophet's virtues, describing the devastation of his death, and ending with Hassan's hope to be reunited with him in Paradise. It remains one of the most recited poems in Islamic devotional literature.

titleAr: ما بال عينك لا تنام كأنما
titleEn: What Ails Your Eye That It Cannot Sleep?

verses.sadr: ما بال عينك لا تنام كأنما

verses.ajuz: يا خير من وطىء الحصى لا تبعد

verses.sadr: أو حل أمر الله فينا عاجلا
verses.ajuz: في روحة من يومنا أو في غد
يا ذا الجلال وذا العلا والسؤدد

translationEn: What ails your eye that it cannot sleep, as though its inner corners were daubed with the kohl of ophthalmia? Grieving for the Guided One, who now lies still — O best of those who ever trod on pebbles, be not far! May my side shield you from the earth — alas, would that I had been buried before you in Baqi' al-Gharqad! By my father and mother, I who witnessed his passing on a Monday — the rightly-guided Prophet. I remained after his death bewildered — O grief of my soul, would that I had never been born! Shall I remain after you in Medina among them? Would that I had drunk the venom of black serpents! Or that God's decree had come upon us swiftly, this very afternoon or tomorrow. Then our Hour would come, and we would meet one whose nature is pure, noble of origin. O firstborn of Amina, blessed in his remembrance — she bore you, a chaste woman, with the best of fortunes. A light that illuminated all of creation — whoever is guided to the blessed light finds true guidance. O Lord! Gather us together with our Prophet in a garden that would turn the eyes of the envious. In the Garden of al-Firdaws, and write it for us, O Lord of Majesty, of Exaltation, and of Sovereignty. By God, as long as I live, I shall never hear of anyone's death without weeping for the Prophet Muhammad. Woe to the Helpers of the Prophet and his kinsmen, after his disappearance into the darkness of the grave. The lands have become narrow for the Ansar, and their faces have turned black like the color of antimony. We bore him and among us is his grave, and the abundance of his blessings upon us is not denied. God honored us through him and guided through him His Helpers at every witnessed hour. May God and those who surround His Throne send blessings, and the righteous ones, upon the blessed Ahmad. The Christians of Yathrib and its Jews rejoiced when he vanished into the niche of the grave.
context: Another major elegy for the Prophet Muhammad, this poem is remarkable for its raw emotional intensity. Hassan wishes he had died before the Prophet, expresses his inability to live in Medina without him, and even wishes for the Hour of Judgment so he could be reunited. The final verse notes bitterly that the non-Muslims of Medina rejoiced at the Prophet's death, sharpening the grief of the believers.

titleAr: آليت ما في جميع الناس مجتهدا ،
titleEn: I Swear with the Utmost Sincerity

verses.sadr: آليت ما في جميع الناس مجتهدا ،
تالله ما حملت أنثى ، ولا وضعت
ولا برا الله خلقا من بريته
من الذي كان نورا يستضاء به ،
يا أفضل الناس ، إني كنت في نهر

translationEn: I swear with the utmost sincerity among all people, a righteous oath, not an empty one. By God, no female has ever carried or given birth to the like of the Prophet, the Messenger of Mercy, the Guide. Nor has God created any creature in all His creation more faithful to a neighbor's covenant or a promise than the one who was a light by which men found illumination, blessed in his affairs, a man of justice and guidance. Confirming the prophets who came before him, and the most generous of people in giving goodness to the seeker. O best of mankind, I was once in a river of abundance, and now I have become like one alone and parched. Your wives have abandoned the furnishing of their homes — they no longer drive tent-pegs above the backs of curtains. Like nuns wearing sackcloth, they have resigned themselves to hardship after the blessings that had been so evident.
context: An elegy for the Prophet Muhammad emphasizing his unique status among all creation. Hassan swears that no woman ever bore a child like Muhammad, and describes the desolation of the Prophet's household after his death. The image of the Prophet's wives in sackcloth like nuns, no longer pitching their curtain-tents, is one of the most moving in Hassan's elegiac poetry.

titleAr: متى يبد في الداجي البهيم جبينه
titleEn: Whenever His Brow Appears in the Pitch-Dark Night

verses.sadr: متى يبد في الداجي البهيم جبينه
فمن كان أو من يكون كأحمد

translationEn: Whenever his brow appears in the pitch-dark night, it gleams like the lamp of darkness, ablaze. Who was or who shall ever be like Ahmad — a pillar of truth, or a scourge for the heretic?
context: A brief but powerful praise fragment for the Prophet, using the classic image of his luminous face illuminating the darkness. The verse captures one of the central themes of Hassan's prophetic praise: Muhammad as both a source of guidance (light for truth) and a force of justice (punishment for heresy).

titleAr: ألا دفنتم رسول الله في سفط
titleEn: Would That You Had Buried the Messenger of God

verses.sadr: ألا دفنتم رسول الله في سفط

translationEn: Would that you had buried the Messenger of God in a casket of aloe-wood and camphor, neatly arranged.
context: A single verse expressing Hassan's wish that the Prophet had been buried in a casket of the finest fragrances. This fragment reflects the deep love and reverence Hassan felt for the Prophet, wishing even his burial to be worthy of his exalted station.

titleEn: Did You Abandon the Frontier and Come Instead
translationEn: Did you abandon the frontier campaigns and come instead to fight a people at the grave of Muhammad? What wretched guidance of the righteous you have followed, and what wretched is the deed of the deliberate ignoramus! If you advance, we shall make the entertainment of your chieftains around Medina every supple, repelling spear. And if you retreat, then what a wretched journey you made, and for the like of your leader's command you found no guidance. It was as though the companions of the Prophet that evening were sacrificial camels being slaughtered at the gate of the mosque. So weep for Abu Amr for his fine service — he now rests in Baqi' al-Gharqad.
context: A poem composed during a period of fitna (civil strife), condemning those who abandoned fighting on the frontiers of Islam to instead wage war near the Prophet's grave in Medina. Hassan's powerful image of the Prophet's companions being slaughtered like sacrificial camels at the mosque gate conveys the horror of Muslim infighting. The poem mourns Abu Amr, a companion buried at Baqi'.

titleAr: ماذا أردتم من أخي الخير باركت
titleEn: What Did You Want from the Man of Goodness?

verses.sadr: ماذا أردتم من أخي الخير باركت
verses.ajuz: يد الله في ذاك الأديم المقدد

verses.sadr: قتلتم ولي الله في جوف داره ،
ألم يك فيكم ذا بلاء ومصدق ،

translationEn: What did you want from the man of goodness — may God's hand bless that dried and leathery skin? You killed the friend of God inside his own house, and brought forth an unjust matter, devoid of guidance. Why did you not observe God's covenant in your midst, and fulfill the pledge — the pledge of Muhammad? Was he not among you a man of fine service and truth, the most faithful of you in keeping covenants at every gathering? May the hands of the people who conspired never prosper — those who collaborated to kill Uthman, the rightly-guided and upright.
context: An elegy for the Caliph Uthman ibn Affan, who was assassinated in 656 CE by rebels in his own home. Hassan, who was loyal to Uthman, condemns the killers for violating the sanctity of his house and breaking the covenant of the Prophet Muhammad. This poem reflects Hassan's political stance during the First Fitna.

titleAr: أمسى الخلابيس قد عزوا وقد كثروا ،
titleEn: The Riffraff Have Grown Mighty and Multiplied

verses.sadr: أمسى الخلابيس قد عزوا وقد كثروا ،

verses.ajuz: أو كان منتشبا في برثن الأسد

verses.sadr: ما البحر حين تهب الريح شامية ،

verses.ajuz: أفري من الغيظ فري العارض البرد
من دية فيه يعطاها ولا قود

verses.sadr: أبلغ عبيدا بأني قد تركت له
verses.ajuz: من خير ما يترك الآباء للولد

translationEn: The riffraff have grown mighty and multiplied, while the son of al-Furay'a has become as exposed as a desert ostrich egg. Muzayna came from the depths to provoke me — begone, Muzayna! My leather straps are around your necks! They walk about whispering threats in their truce, threatening me as if I were nobody. May his mother be bereft of whoever I find, or whoever is caught in the lion's claw! The sea, when the north wind blows and it surges and hurls foam across its surface, is not on any day more overwhelming than I when you see me, tearing with rage like a hailstorm cloud that rips and shreds. The slain one whom I rise to take has no blood-money to be paid for him, and no retaliation. Tell Ubayd that I have left him the best of what fathers leave to their children: a spacious house, and palm trees lining the way, and fair women trailing their garments like striped cloaks.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ostrich egg of the desert (exposed, alone)

context: A poem of self-defense and boasting in which Hassan responds to threats from the tribe of Muzayna. He compares himself to a raging sea and a lion, warning his enemies that those he targets receive neither blood-money nor legal retaliation. The closing verses, addressed to his son Ubayd, leave him a legacy of home, palms, and family — a touching mix of defiance and paternal tenderness.

titleEn: Is There One Who Will Convey to Rabi'a from Me?
translationEn: Is there one who will convey to Rabi'a from me — what have you done since the events after my time? Your father was the man of deeds, Abu Bara', and your maternal uncle the glorious Hakam ibn Sa'd. O sons of Umm al-Banin! Were you not alarmed — you who are the forelock of the people of Najd? Amir mocked Abu Bara' to betray his protection, and an intentional wrong is not like an accident.
context: This poem addresses the sons of Rabi'a, reminding them of their noble father Abu Bara' and demanding they avenge the treachery of Amir, who violated Abu Bara's guarantee of protection. The incident refers to the tragedy of Bi'r Ma'una, where Muslim missionaries were massacred despite being under Abu Bara's protection. Hassan's verse draws a sharp moral line: deliberate betrayal is far worse than error.

titleEn: Did the Sons of the Foundling Woman Rejoice?
translationEn: Did the sons of the foundling woman rejoice that we were at peace on the morning of al-Miqdad's cavalry? We were eight, and they were a vast throng, yet they scattered by the spears in all directions. Were it not for what the horses suffered and the rubbing of their hooves to the south of Saya yesterday at al-Taqwad — their hind hooves worn away and their backs parched, a day of being led and a day of pursuit — we would have met you with them carrying every fully-armored warrior, defender of honor, glorious of ancestry. We were among the vanguard who face you, when you cast away the reins of every steed. Nay, by the Lord of the she-camels racing toward Mina and those crossing the mountain passes! Until we stable our horses in your courtyards and return with the queens and children. Proudly upon every lean, swift stallion and mare, in every battlefield they have turned and charged. They were in their abode enjoying ease, but were transformed — on the days of Dhu Qarad — into the faces of slaves.
context: A boastful poem recounting a military encounter where a small Muslim force under al-Miqdad scattered a much larger enemy force. Hassan refers to the expedition of Dhu Qarad (also called Ghazwat Dhu Qarad), a pursuit mission. The poem mocks the enemy's ignoble lineage and celebrates the Muslim warriors' prowess despite being vastly outnumbered.

titleEn: Look, My Friend, in the Heart of Jilliq

verses.sadr: جمال شعثاء قد هبطن من ال
يحملن حوا ، حور المدامع في الر
من دون بصرى ، وخلفها جبل الثلج
ما حلت عن خير ما عهدت ، ولا
تقول شعثاء : لو تفيق من الكأ
أهوى حديث الندمان في فلق الصب

verses.ajuz: يخاف جاري ما عشت من وبد

translationEn: Look, my friend, in the heart of Jilliq — can you make out anyone beyond al-Balqa'? The camels of Sha'tha' have descended from the halt between the sand dunes and the hillside. They carry dark-skinned beauties with wide, lustrous eyes in the litter, and white-faced women like hailstones. Beyond Busra, and behind it the Snow Mountain, upon which the clouds lie like strips of leather. I swear by the Lord of the humbled she-camels and what they cross of every hard, barren plain, and by the sacrificial camels when they are brought near for slaughter — an oath of a righteous man, sworn with full sincerity: I have not departed from the best of what I knew, nor have I loved anyone as I love you. Sha'tha' says: If you would sober up from the wine, you would find yourself wealthy in possessions. I love the conversation of boon companions at the crack of dawn, and the voice of the melodious night-companion. But the sword and the tongue refuse me idleness, and a people unoppressed, like the mane of the lion. I do not scratch my drinking companion, nor does my sitting companion fear my hand when I am intoxicated. My drinking companion is never a biting miser, nor does my neighbor fear any harm from me as long as I live.
context: One of Hassan's most famous pre-Islamic poems, set in the landscape of Syria and Jordan. Hassan recalls his journeys to the Ghassanid court in Damascus, describing the beauty of women traveling in camel-litters and the snowcapped mountains of Syria. The poem shifts to a self-portrait of Hassan as a generous, harmless drinking companion and a warrior-poet. It reveals the poet's life before Islam, when he was patronized by the Ghassanid kings.

titleEn: Convey to All Who Listen of a Battle

verses.ajuz: على أي حال كان حام وذائد
ولا طاف لي منهم بوحشي صائد

verses.sadr: وأن لم يزل لي منذ أدركت كاشح ،

verses.ajuz: بمثل له مثلين ، أو أنا زائد

verses.sadr: أنا الزائر الصقر ابن سلمى ، وعنده

verses.ajuz: وعمي ابن هند مطعم الطير خالد

verses.sadr: ومنا قتيل الشعب أوس بن ثابت ،
ومن جده الأدنى أبي ، وابن أمه

verses.ajuz: أذاة ، ولا مزر به ، وهو عائد

verses.sadr: فمهما أقل مما أعدد لم يزل
verses.ajuz: على صدقه من كل قومي شاهد

verses.sadr: متى ما نسم لا ينكر الناس وسمنا ،

verses.ajuz: كما لاح في سمر المتان الموارد
ويسعدن في الدنيا بنا من نساعد

verses.sadr: إذا ما كسرنا رمح راية شاعر ،

verses.ajuz: ولاح شهاب من سنا الحرب واقد

verses.sadr: ثلاثة أيام من الدهر لم يكن

translationEn: Convey to all who listen of a battle at which grey-haired old women would leap up in alarm. They believe of me that I am for my kinsmen, in whatever state, a protector and defender. If I do not confirm their belief with certainty, then may the thunderclouds never water my bones. My peers among men know that I am the horseman who protects the sacred things, the valorous contender. My enemies have found no weakness in me, nor has any hunter among them stalked my flank. Since I came of age, I have always had a hateful foe I struggle against, and another envious one. There is none of them but I repay him with the like of his deed twofold, or I give more. If you ask the tribes about me, I belong to a lineage to which all noble lineages aspire. I am the one who visited al-Saqr ibn Salma, and with him my father, Nu'man, Amr, and Wafid. He bequeathed me glory, and whoever reaps such things from where he reaped them returns giving praise. My grandfather was the orator of the people on the day of Sumayha, and my uncle Khalid ibn Hind was the one who fed the birds of battle. Among us is the martyr of the mountain pass, Aws ibn Thabit, a witness, and the most exalted remembrance from the battlefields. And from his nearest grandfather — my father — and his brother by my father's mother, that martyr and warrior. In every household there is a Khazrajite lady, and an Awsite lady, among whose shelter I have a father. None of us prepares harm for his neighbor, nor does he bring upon him reproach while visiting. For we regard the right of the neighbor as a trust, and the noble covenant-keeper among us preserves it. Whatever I say of what I recount, there has always been from every one of my people a witness to its truth. Every people has a brand-mark by which they are known, and our brand-mark among us is the everlasting poems. Whenever we brand, people do not deny our mark, and through it we identify the unknown among those we oppose. Our mark shines and people flock to it, as watering-marks gleam on the tawny flanks of hills. They heal those whose healing cannot be achieved, and they endure as long as the eternal mountains endure. They bring misery to those who assail us with enmity, and they bring fortune in this world to those we aid. When we break the spear-shaft of a rival poet's banner, our reserves surge forth and we resume. When he spreads satire against his people and a firebrand from the blaze of war glows bright, he becomes like the most wretched of Thamud, who took upon himself for his doom to hamstring the mother of the young camel, while the calf was coming to drink. He then turned and climbed thoughtfully to the top of a boulder whose summit grew tall and whose foundations were firm. He said: Enjoy yourselves in your homes, for a warning and a promise have come to you — three days of time, for which there was no messenger to confirm what he said.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: young camel (from the story of Salih)

context: This is Hassan's grand fakhr (boasting) qasida, one of the longest and most elaborate self-praise poems in his diwan. He traces his noble genealogy through both Khazraj and Aws, boasts of his warrior ancestors, and declares that poetry is the eternal brand-mark of his people. The final section invokes the Quranic story of Thamud to warn rival poets that attacking Hassan's tribe will bring divine-scale destruction upon them.

titleAr: تروح من الحسناء أم أنت مغتدي ،
titleEn: Do You Depart from the Beauty or Do You Stay?

verses.sadr: تروح من الحسناء أم أنت مغتدي ،
وعبسا على ما في الأديم الممدد

verses.ajuz: من الظلم في الأحلاف حمل التغمد

verses.sadr: إذا المرء لم يفضل ، ولم يلق نجدة
كثير المنى بالزاد ، لا خير عنده
verses.ajuz: إذا جاع يوما يشتكيه ضحى الغد

translationEn: Do you depart from the beauty at evening or set out at dawn? How can a lover travel without provisions? She appeared to us on the day of departure with the eyes of a gazelle in a dense thicket of lote-trees, alone. And a neck like the neck of an oryx, pure, adorned by the gleam of rubies and the setting of emeralds. As though the Pleiades above the hollow of her throat were blazing in the darkness — what a blazing! She has walls of death below her, and a host that when Yathrib cries out, rises up. You see the black lava-field turn red in its color. By my life, I have allied with all of Dhubyan and Abs upon what is written on the outstretched parchment. I came from the land of the Hijaz with a company that fills the open ground like scattered sandgrouse. I bore what Muzayna used to complain of — the burden of wrongs in the alliances, the bearing of silent endurance. I see that abundant generosity bequeaths to its people honor, and the wretches of evil times are ennobled who were never noble. If a man does not excel and does not show bravery with his people, let him sit in humiliation and be gone. I am the richest of people beyond needing a pretender who sees people as astray while he himself is unguided. One full of wishes about provisions, with no good in him — when he goes hungry one day, he complains of it the next morning. He grew up ignorant, barren, wretched, accursed, stubborn as though his head were the head of a proud stag.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a proud stag with a stiff neck

context: A composite poem that begins with a ghazal opening describing a beautiful woman's departure, transitions into tribal politics involving alliances with Dhubyan and Abs, and ends with a sharp hija against a pretentious rival. The poem reflects Hassan's pre-Islamic milieu and the complex web of tribal alliances in the Hijaz and Najd.

titleAr: لعمر أبيك الخير ، يا شعث ، ما نبا
titleEn: By Your Good Father's Life, O Sha'th

verses.sadr: لعمر أبيك الخير ، يا شعث ، ما نبا
verses.ajuz: علي لساني ، في الخطوب ، ولا يدي
ويبلغ ما لا يبلغ السيف مذودي

verses.sadr: وإن أك ذا مال قليل أجد به ،
verses.ajuz: وإن يهتصر عودي على الجهد يحمد
وأهلا ، إذا ما جاء من غير مرصد
إذا حل عنها رحلها لم تقيد
تروح إلى باب ابن سلمى ، وتغتدي
جوادا متى يذكر له الخير يزدد

verses.sadr: فلا تعجلن يا قيس واربع ، فإنما

verses.ajuz: متى ترهم يا بن الخطيم تبلد
وأنت لدى الكنات في كل مطرد
وزند متى تقدح به النار يصلد

translationEn: By your good father's life, O Sha'th, neither my tongue nor my hand has ever failed me in crises. My tongue and my sword are both sharp, and my poetic lance reaches where the sword cannot. If I have little wealth, I am generous with it, and if my branch is bent by hardship, it is still praised. Neither wealth makes me forget my modesty and honor, nor do the blows of fate dull my file. My family is larger than other men's dependents, yet I endure on cold, pure water. I give whatever I find, and I say to the one who lights my fire on a windy night: Light it up! I am one who says 'Welcome' and 'Be at home' to the grieving visitor when he comes unannounced. When generosity calls me, I answer it, and I strike the gleaming, blazing white of lightning. I am sweet, though bitterness may touch me, and I am one who abandons what I have not been accustomed to. I drive my mounts onward through pain of foot, and I forsake the comfortable, well-made bed. I work the strong she-camel until I bring her back so exhausted that when her saddle is removed, she cannot be hobbled. I task her to travel through the entire night, heading to the door of Ibn Salma morning and evening. I found him an ocean overflowing with bounty, a generous man who grows in goodness the more it is mentioned. So do not be hasty, O Qays, and pause, for your utmost fate is to be met with every Indian sword — keen blades and spears in the hands of mighty men. Whenever you see them, O son of al-Khutaym, you will be struck dumb. Lions whose cubs defend their lair, thrusting with Khatti spears at every gathering. The Aws have tasted battle and been driven out, while you were hiding behind the women's curtains in every pursuit. So fondle the soft, fair-eyed beauties at your doors, and line your pretty eyes with kohl. A base mother weaned you away from glory, and a fire-steel that gives no spark whenever it is struck.
context: A masterful fakhr qasida combining self-praise, praise of a patron (Ibn Salma), and fierce satire against the Aws tribe and a man called Qays ibn al-Khutaym. Hassan portrays himself as the ideal Arab: generous despite poverty, brave in battle, eloquent in verse, and hospitable to all visitors. The poem ends with devastating insults against his Awsite rivals, mocking their cowardice and ignoble mothers. This reflects the pre-Islamic rivalry between the Khazraj and Aws tribes of Medina.

titleAr: ومن عاش منا عاش في عنجهية
titleEn: Whoever Among Us Lives, Lives in Proud Defiance

verses.sadr: ومن عاش منا عاش في عنجهية

translationEn: Whoever among us lives, lives in proud defiance, enduring the hardship of a wretched livelihood.
context: A single verse of tribal boasting, likely a fragment from a longer poem. Hassan declares that his people live with unbreakable pride even in the harshest conditions, a common theme in pre-Islamic and early Islamic poetry where endurance of hardship is a mark of nobility.
